steps.title: Identify what the question needs
steps.content: The passage explains that traveling waves break — and are height-constrained — once steepness exceeds a critical threshold. The key finding is that spike waves can exceed that constraint. The question asks what the passage most strongly supports about spike waves.

steps.title: Eliminate the choices
steps.content:
• Choice A — McAllister et al. suggest that spike waves can form when traveling waves propagating in opposing directions intersect and that spike waves tend to be higher than traveling waves.: oversimplifies the finding; the passage's point is the violation of the expected limit, not a blanket claim about size. ✗
  • Choice B — The process of breaking limits the height of traveling waves, but the study by McAllister et al. suggests that spike waves can exceed those limits if their height-to-length ratio reaches a critical threshold.: implies steepness still limits spike waves, which is the opposite of the passage's point. ✗
  • Choice C — The study by McAllister et al. suggests that when traveling waves intersect in specific ways, the resulting wave may be higher than would be expected based on the properties of traveling waves.: directly captures that spike waves exceed the height constraint that governs traveling waves. ✓
  • Choice D — Previous studies have suggested that steepness mediates breaking in traveling waves, but the study by McAllister et al. shows that jet stability and cavity shape may also influence breaking in such waves.: shifts focus to traveling waves rather than the exceptional behavior of spike waves. ✗
That matches Choice C — The study by McAllister et al. suggests that when traveling waves intersect in specific ways, the resulting wave may be higher than would be expected based on the properties of traveling waves..
